A shared design toolkit for the stewardship of publicly owned land in your local housing estate.

Boundary conditions:

What kind of boundary are you looking at? Is it stopping you and your local community from using your outside space that could be collectively enjoyed, cared for, or shared?

Check:

What authority is the land under?

This project is interested in the overlap between 'publicly owned land' and housing association or estate authority. Click here for a map.

What is the railing doing?

What conditions is it separating? Road? Playground? Grass? This project is interested in undermining the authority of the railings that are…

Attachment strategies:

Before considering devices for crossing railings, look at how things already attach to them.

crossrailings.org

Is it wider at the top and bottom? »

Does it have round bars? »

Crossing devices

Once the boundary is understood, and the fixings are decided, build a device for crossing.

These designs invite access into the underused spaces around you.

Select a crossing device below to see how to build it, or submit your own design here »

crossrailings.org

How to build:
side stair

A simple side mounted stair device for crossing over low/medium-height railings.

to prepare:

Gather a small group!

First things first, survey your railing:
For this you will need a tape measure and somewhere to note down dimensions.

  • Measure the whole height (A)
  • Measure the distance between the top and bottom railing (B)
  • Measure the length of the railing bay (C)
tap to zoom

This will give you your limits, and match up your railing to the design.

You will need (or equivalent):

FYI, everything is in dimensions that could be marked / measured with a common 30cm ruler. Get timber cut at the supplier where possible.

25×50mm timber battens (suggest weather treated red graded)
These come in lengths of up to 4.8m, you might need up to 10.
– 4 × 300mm long
– 16 × 600mm long
– 4 × 750mm long
– 4 × 900mm long
– 4 × 1350mm long
– 4 × 1500mm long
– 4 × 1950mm long
  • Anti-slip tape for the contact zones between metal and timber
  • 6 × 600×600mm plywood/OSB board for the stair platforms
  • A pack of cable ties for fastening to the railing
  • Use 4mm diameter × 50mm long exterior wood screws for standard batten-to-batten joints. Pre-drill pilot holes, especially near the ends. Use two screws per load-bearing joint where possible. Get a big pack.

Tools

If you can't get your timber pieces cut at the supplier, go to a local workshop. See if you can borrow a drill and drill bits there too. If not, you can rent from your local tool library.

You will need a drill on site.

build stages:

This device can be assembled on the ground outside. Lay the side frames flat first, then raise them in pairs and connect them together. Work in a small group where possible.

click to zoom

01 — gather and prepare all your pieces

That should look a bit like this:

02 — lay out your side frames

repeat ×4

03 — drill holes and screw the side frames together

Pre-drill pilot holes through the top batten. Fix with 2 × exterior wood screws per joint, each approximately 4mm diameter × 50mm long. Keep screws at least 20–25mm away from the end of the timber to reduce splitting.

04 — raise the frames in pairs

Many hands make light work! This step needs at least 2 people.

prop frames with blocks while fixing

Fix each cross batten with a screw at each junction. Again, pre-drill holes first! — and repeat for the other pair.

05 — add step boards

Fix each step board with your screws. Use one in each corner of the board. Pre-drill where needed.

06 — brace the frame

Diagonal bracing stops the frame from twisting. 2 screws at each end.

07 — tape, pair and cable tie up!

Tape the timber where it will be in most contact with the railing, to reduce slipping.

Place the two frames on either side of the railing with the bracing on the outside.

Cable tie them to each other, trapping the railings in between.

08 — test before crossing

Pull, shake, and load gradually before crossing. Test weight before full use.

09 — occupy and enjoy!
